turns.content: Why does this even work this way? I'm trying to split my log files into two buckets based on severity, but the standard iterators just pass everything through. I expected a built-in partition function like in some scripting languages where you'd just call filter or map and get the lists back immediately.

That's a common frustration when coming from scripting backgrounds because Rust doesn't expose a top-level 'partition' function on iterables itself. Instead, you implement the logic inline using iterators. You can use filter to create two separate collections by chaining them with map or collect methods, but there isn't a single function that returns both lists simultaneously without manual handling.

Okay so I have to manually split the iterator? That feels clunky compared to just calling one function and getting two outputs. Is there any way to reuse the original data structure instead of creating copies?

You are right that it feels clunky, but Rust's ownership model means you cannot safely return multiple iterators from a single source without consuming them. If you need to preserve the original collection or avoid copying large data structures, you can use partitioning logic within an iterator chain where the predicate determines which bucket each element goes into during traversal.
I see the pattern now but I'm still stuck on how to actually implement this without writing verbose loops. Is there a crate that provides idiomatic helpers for grouping or partitioning?

Yes, the itertools crate is your best friend here. It provides group_by which groups elements by their key, and you can combine it with other utilities like fold or map to achieve what you need without writing manual loops.
Wait, so I shouldn't use partition? But I thought partition was a standard pattern in Rust collections?

You have a misconception here. Partition is not a standard method on Vec or iterators in the Rust standard library itself; it's an idiom you implement using filter chains. The itertools crate does provide group_by which groups by key, but if your goal is strictly splitting into two buckets based on a predicate, you can achieve this elegantly with just filter and map without needing to import anything extra.

I'm struggling to build a multimap where I can look up a key that appears multiple times by its index. My current approach uses a HashMap with Vec values, but it feels too slow when I need to partition the input list first. Here's my draft logic: let mut map = std::collections::HashMap::new(); for (i, item) in items.iter().enumerate() { map.entry(item.key).or_insert(Vec::new()).push((i, item)); } Is there a better way using itertools or by leveraging partition patterns to avoid the inner loop overhead?

The bottleneck is likely how you're handling the insertion into the HashMap. Instead of iterating through every element with an index, consider using itertools::Partition on your iterator to separate elements based on a predicate before grouping them. This shifts the work from a per-element check during iteration to a pre-filtered collection. Think of it like sorting a messy desk: if you first sweep all papers into two piles (kept vs discarded) instead of checking each one while placing it, you avoid constantly reaching across the table. For building your multimap specifically, itertools::GroupBy can group entries by their key, allowing you to then index or sort those groups efficiently without redundant predicate checks on every item.
